video_title: Hiring Has Expanded Beyond Healthcare, Richardson Says
transcript: Let's talk about this labor market. How tight are things? What do you see? We had a really decent ADP print early this week. In fact, when I saw the number, I thought immediately of of Lisa because she has been asking me for several months now, is this employment picture broadening out? Is the hiring pattern broadening past health care? And, yes, this month, it did. Good. This was the first month we've seen in years with this broad of a hiring pattern across eight out of 10 supersectors. Even manufacturing for the first time in over two years saw positive job gains, and we saw gains on all all size fronts in terms of the establishment sizes. But to your question on tightness, if you look at the wage data, it ain't tight yet. And so the quality of the new jobs, different beast than the quality of jobs two years ago, that means lower pay growth, not higher. And so when we look at job changer pay growth and we can match them with each individual for each cohort, it's down a bit from last month. Six and a half percent, that's not bad, but it's not something that bends in any way towards a wage price spiral. You talk about changing in the composition of the the types of jobs that are getting created. Can you give some color around that? Which jobs are being created and which aren't that would lead to more robust wage growth? So because we're tracking paychecks, we actually know how much, how many hours people are working. And from that, we can calculate the part time share of new jobs and the ex just existing headcount for all jobs. And what we're seeing is that before the pandemic, part time share was about 37%. It's gone up five percentage points. It's now 42% of all the jobs we're seeing in the payroll data are part time. And then if you look at health care, the jobs that are being produced there remember, health care is a stalwart. It's producing still the bulk of job gains. Those jobs are tending towards home health care aids and lower paying health care jobs for the sector. And then if you look at the size cohort, it's the young, the smaller firms that are creating the bulk of jobs. Over half of the jobs created in the ADP report were for firms with under 50 in headcount, and they tend to have slower pay growth. So all in all, this is a lower wage dynamic than we saw before the pandemic. You know, you talk about part time jobs, and it's something that people talk about when we get these jobs numbers, the job growth numbers. They say that they're overly inflated because they reflect people getting multiple jobs. They reflect someone having to work three different jobs to support their family. How much is that what we're seeing in the headline numbers and, frankly, even from some of the job creation? Well, if you look at the part time chair, that suggests that it's more than one job if you're thinking about supporting a full life. Right? So I I think that multiple jobs, gig work is really important. And it's not just young people who are doing multiple jobs. It's older workers. It's workers who are retirement age and now have a ten ninety nine, a side business. We used to say back in the day, a side hustle. That's something New Yorkers are very familiar with. That is common and growing in the retirement age, older age workers. So this is not going to be an unusual aspect in a very expensive economy. Affordability is a factor, and it's leading to a lot more multiple jobs.
